coredata.dc:title: N-doped porous reduced graphene oxide as an efficient electrode material for high performance flexible solid-state supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Santosh K.
Dhavale, Vishal M.
Boukherroub, Rabah
Kurungot, Sreekumar
Szunerits, Sabine
coredata.dc:description: Supercapacitors (SCs) are energy storage devices with an immense potential to resolve energy related issues. Being a low cost, durable and multifunctional material, graphene and its derivatives are the most promising candidates for SCs. Here, we report the synthesis of N-doped porous reduced graphene (N-pGr) and its use for the fabrication of all-solid-state supercapacitor (ASSC) device. The N-pGr exhibited a specific capacitance of 230Fg−1 at 1Ag−1 current density and good capacitance retention up to ∼88% even after 10,000 galvanostatic charge–discharge cycles recorded at 5Ag−1 fabricated in a solid-state supercapacitor. The synergistic effect of the porosity and N-doping is believed to be the underlying principle for the improved charge storage ability when compared to other reports. Significantly, the porous nature of N-pGr contributes in increasing the electroactive surface area for the formation of electrical double layer as well as facilitating the faster movement of electrolyte ions inside the electrode network. N-doping is believed to change the electrical properties of the electrode material and also to participate in the pseudo-capacitance which helped to boost the overall capacitance of the fabricated device. The good capacitance retention provides the room for making robust flexible solid-state supercapacitor (FSSC) with the N-pGr material.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped porous reduced graphene
Flexible solid-state supercapacitor
Energy storage
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids confeditor contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb vol volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of porous reduced graphene (pGr) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Synthesis of N-doped porous reduced graphene (N-pGr)
Formation and characterization of porous N-doped reduced graphene (N-pGr)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis of N-doped porous graphene (N-pGr) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and their potential application for the fabrication of ASSC device.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of (a) reduced graphene oxide (rGO), (b) porous reduced graphene oxide (pGr), (c) pGr image at high resolution, (d) uniformly distributed pores over the porous N-doped reduced graphene oxide (N-pGr), (e) N-pGr image at high resolution (Inset: SAED pattern showing semi-crystalline nature of N-pGr), and (f) uniformly distributed graphene quantum dots (GQDs) of size range ∼3–5nm, which are generated during oxidation process of rGO (Inset: magnified image of GQD).

BET surface area analysis and pore size distribution of rGO and N-pGr samples. (a) Comparative N2 adsorption–desorption curve for the rGO and N-pGr, (b) comparative pore size distribution for rGO and N-pGr, (c) and (d) Langmuir fits from the N2 adsorption data for rGO and N-pGr. Solid squares are experimental data and line is fitting data.

Physical characterizations of samples: (a) Comparative XRD spectra of Gr, pGr, and N-pGr, (b) Raman spectra of Gr, pGr, and N-pGr, (c) comparative survey scan XPS spectra of pGr and N-pGr, (d) comparative deconvoluted C1s spectrum of N-pGr, and pGr sample, and (e) deconvoluted N1s spectrum of N-pGr sample.

Electrochemical performance of the ASSC devices: (a) Comparative CVs of the electrodes fabricated by using rGO, pGr and N-pGr at a scan rate of 10mVs−1, (b) CV of the electrode fabricated with N-pGr recorded at different scan rates, (c) comparative Nyquist plots of the electrodes fabricated with rGO, pGr and N-pGr, and (d–f) charge–discharge (CD) profiles recorded at different current densities, capacitance retention with respect to the change in charge–discharge current density and cycling durability test measured for 10,000 cycles at 5Ag−1 current density (Inset: optical image of lightening of LED upon connection with 3-ASSC devices in series), respectively, of the electrodes fabricated with N-pGr.

Electrochemical testing of the ASSC device fabricated with N-pGr. (a) Plots of the energy density and power density as a function of charge–discharge current density and (b) demonstration of flexibility of the SSC device with different bending states.
Comparison of the electrochemical performance of some of the N-enriched carbon materials.

head.ce:title: N-doped porous reduced graphene oxide as an efficient electrode material for high performance flexible solid-state supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physical and Materials Chemistry Division, CSIR-National Chemical Laboratory, Dr. HomiBhabha Road, Pune 411008, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Physical and Materials Chemistry Division, CSIR-National Chemical Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research, AnusandhanBhawan, 2 RafiMarg, New Delhi 110 001, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research, AnusandhanBhawan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tokyo Institute of Technology, Laboratory for Chemistry and Life Science Institute of Innovative Research, R1-17, 4259 Nagatsuta, Midori-ku, Yokohama 226-8503, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tokyo Institute of Technology, Laboratory for Chemistry and Life Science Institute of Innovative Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Univ. Lille, CNRS, Centrale Lille, ISEN, Univ. Valenciennes, UMR 8520 - IEMN, F-59000 Lille, France

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Physical and Materials Chemistry Division, CSIR-National Chemical Laboratory, Dr. HomiBhabha Road, Pune 411008, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Physical and Materials Chemistry Division, CSIR-National Chemical Laboratory

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supercapacitors (SCs) are energy storage devices with an immense potential to resolve energy related issues. Being a low cost, durable and multifunctional material, graphene and its derivatives are the most promising candidates for SCs. Here, we report the synthesis of N-doped porous reduced graphene (N-pGr) and its use for the fabrication of all-solid-state supercapacitor (ASSC) device. The N-pGr exhibited a specific capacitance of 230Fg−1 at 1Ag−1 current density and good capacitance retention up to ∼88% even after 10,000 galvanostatic charge–discharge cycles recorded at 5Ag−1 fabricated in a solid-state supercapacitor. The synergistic effect of the porosity and N-doping is believed to be the underlying principle for the improved charge storage ability when compared to other reports. Significantly, the porous nature of N-pGr contributes in increasing the electroactive surface area for the formation of electrical double layer as well as facilitating the faster movement of electrolyte ions inside the electrode network. N-doping is believed to change the electrical properties of the electrode material and also to participate in the pseudo-capacitance which helped to boost the overall capacitance of the fabricated device. The good capacitance retention provides the room for making robust flexible solid-state supercapacitor (FSSC) with the N-pGr material.

ce:section.ce:para: The global energy demand is increasing exponentially [1–6], and the major part of it is being provided by fossil fuels [5,7–10]. The depletion of fossil fuels and their hazardous environmental impact are knocking the doors of scientific community to develop clean and eco-friendly energy sources [9,11]. In view of this, electrochemical energy sources such as batteries and supercapacitors (SCs) are gaining elevated importance as efficient energy storage devices [2,4,12,13]. These energy devices are able to convert chemical energy into electrical energy without creating any hazardous environmental impact with long life cycle [2,4,9]. Specifically, the batteries store energy via complex metal-ion intercalation–deintercalation mechanism, and SCs through the simple surface charge accumulation [12,14,15]. Moreover, the performance of latter one can be tuned by modulation in the electrode–electrolyte interface [12,16–18].

Looking into the literature, various kinds of metals, metal oxides, conducting polymers and their composites have been identified to have good charge storage ability [6,19–24]. Most of the reports are however testing the performance of supercapacitors in the liquid state. For future practical applications, we need to think of the solid state devices, which can easily overcome the additional issue of electrolyte leakage. Recently, Bihag et al. reported on the CNF composite with PEDOT as a solid state supercapacitor and found specific capacitance of 177Fg−1 with reasonable stability [25]. Furthermore, they demonstrated the flexible solid-state supercapacitor based on PEDOT-coated butter paper possessing good specific capacitance with high retention [26]. Looking closer into these reports, we found that the morphology of the electrode material is playing a major role to achieve desired ion shuttling. The intrinsic porosity of the electrode is thus a major and important characteristic to obtain enhanced charge storage ability. In light of this, different carbon materials have been explored for SCs application reported reasonable capacitance [1,27].

The use of graphene based materials as electrode matrices for the construction of SCs has shown to be of high advantage over other materials due to their good mechanical strength, high conductivity, large surface area etc. [28–31]. Recently, porous graphene and heteroatom-doped graphene-based composites have been proposed as efficient electrode materials for SCs [32,33]. The proposed structures increased the active surface area, while the introduction of defects and heteroatom doping are changing the electro-neutrality of graphene and thus allow fast access of ions and electrolytes to the electrode resulting in increased pseudo-capacitance, fast charging–discharging rates and high charge storage capacity [34,35]. Kaner et al. fabricated high performance flexible solid state supercapacitors by using laser scribed reduced graphene oxide (rGO) as electrode material [36,37]. The performance of the supercapacitors based on graphene has been further improved by using chemically modified rGO as an electrode material (functional group modification, hetero-atom doping, surface area modification etc.) [33,34,38–41]. Furthermore, the synthesis of mesoporous graphene nanoballs (MGB) using CVD method with a specific capacitance of 206Fg−1 and good capacitance retention after the charge–discharge (CD) cycling stability test has been recently demonstrated by Jang et al. [33]. The obtained high capacitance was attributed to the good accessibility of electrolyte ions to active sites due to the porous nature of MGBs [33]. Furthermore, by modification of active surface area of graphene using different synthesis approaches, many reports have appeared in a short period of time [34,35,42,43]. Overall, in carbon materials, the conductivity and intrinsic porosity are very important factors. In view of this, herein we explore the synthesis of porous N-doped graphene (N-pGr) material possessing inherent porosity. The prepared N-pGr has shown a good capacitance behaviour in the fabricated all solid-state supercapacitor device and flexible solid state device. The fabricated device displayed retained capacitance value in various deformed conditions with excellent cyclic stability. Hence, the prepared N-pGr can find enormous importance in the field of energy devices.

Graphite powder, phosphoric acid (H3PO4), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), hydrazine monohydrate (N2H4·H2O), liquid ammonia, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (M.W 115,000; 98−99mol% hydrolyzed) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich and were used without any further purification. Polypropylene membranes were procured from Celgard® and used as separators. The Grafoil® carbon paper procured from Graftech was used as a current collector.

Reduced graphene oxide was synthesized by the treatment of reduced graphene oxide (GO) with hydrazine monohydrate. In brief, GO was synthesized by following a modified Hummer's method, in which graphite powder and KMnO4 were mixed and grounded well together in the ratio of 1:6 to get a uniform mixture. The above obtained mixture was added drop wise to H2SO4:H3PO4 (9:1) by keeping the temperature of the solution cool using ice bath. The obtained uniformly dispersed solution was further heated in an oil bath for 24h at 60°C. After the completion of reaction, the obtained solution was poured gradually into an ice bath containing 30% H2O2 with continuous stirring. After complete mixing of the above solution, a light yellow coloured solution was obtained. The formed GO was first precipitated through centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 10min and then washed several times with copious amount of distilled water. The obtained precipitate was further washed with 30% hydrochloric acid to remove unreacted metal residues. The finally obtained material was washed with copious amount of ethanol and diethyl ether (three times) and dried at 60°C for 12h. To the prepared GO (3mgmL−1) aqueous solution, hydrazine was added 1mLhydrazine/mLGOsolution and kept stirring at 80°C for 12h in an oil bath. The resulting rGO was separated by filtration and washed with adequate amount of water to get rid of the unreacted hydrazine and dried at 60°C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of porous reduced graphene (pGr) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of pGr and GQDs was achieved according to a procedure reported recently by some of us [44,45]. In Brief, 100mg of rGO powder was dispersed in 100mL of 30% H2O2 and ultrasonicated for 30min. The obtained uniformly dispersed solution was kept refluxing for 12h at 60°C. The resulting solution was filtered and the GQDs separated from pGr. The obtained pGr powder as well as the GQDs was further dialyzed to remove H2O2 and to separate rGO from small sized GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped porous reduced graphene (N-pGr)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of N-pGr material was achieved by following hydrothermal method in which pGr powder and liquid ammonia (1:30w/v) were mixed together by ultra-sonication before being transferred to a 50mL Teflon® coated stainless steel (SS) autoclave and heated for 24h at 200°C. The acquired solution was separated by filtration followed by washing with ethanol/water (1:1) mixture solution to completely remove the ammonia. The product was kept for drying in the oven at 60°C overnight.

The ASSC device was fabricated by using Grafoil® carbon electrode coated with active material maintaining the loading of 1mgcm−2. Moreover, the active material coated Grafoil® electrode has been achieved by coating an ink made by mixing the active material with NMP onto the 1cm−2 area of the Grafoil®. For making the electrode, Grafoil® was taken with an extra size (1×2.5cm−2) so that after coating of active material excess part can be utilized for the connection purpose during the electrochemical measurement. The obtained active material coated Grafoil electrode was further kept for drying in oven at 60°C for overnight.

The ASSC devices were then fabricated by sandwiching PVA-H2SO4 gel (prepared by mixing 1g of PVA powder in 10mL of 10% w/w aqueous solution of H2SO4) electrolyte between active materials coated electrodes separated by Celgard® membrane. The fabricated ASSC device was dried at 60°C for 6h and used for the analysis. The fabricated single ASSC device was having an operating potential of 1V. To make a real demonstration of the ASSC device, 3 devices were connected in series to get a single device with an operating potential 3V. The obtained 3V device was charged by using a potentiostat and connected to a LED which operates at 2.5V.

1mg of the sample was dispersed in 5mL of isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and 5μL of the resulting dispersed solution was drop coated on a 200 mesh Cu grid for the high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) analysis. The material coated Cu grid was dried and the obtained sample was used for imaging. For the TEM imaging purposes, a FEI, TECNAI G2 F20 instrument operating at an accelerated voltage of 200kV (C s =0.6mm, resolution 1.7Å) was used. For the BET surface area and pore size distribution analyses, low-pressure volumetric gas adsorption measurements were used at 77K and maintained by a liquid-nitrogen bath on a Quantachrome Quadrasorb automatic volumetric instrument. XRD analysis of the prepared samples was carried out by using a Rigaku Smart Lab X-ray diffractometer in reflection mode using CuKα radiation (λ =1.54Å). For the XRD analysis, samples were scanned between the 2θ ranges from 10° to 80° at a scan rate of 2°min−1. A VG MicrotechMultilab ESCA 3000 spectrometer equipped with an Mg Kα-X-ray source (hν =1.2536keV) was used for the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of the samples. For the Raman spectroscopic analysis, a HR 800 Raman spectrometer (JobinYvon, Horiba, France) equipped with a 632nm red laser (NRS 1500W) was used.

All the electrochemical experiments were performed by using the ModuLab-MTS electrochemical Test Station (Solartron, France). Electrochemical impedance spectrometry (EIS) measurements were performed with an AutolabGalvanostat-Potentiostat instrument between 10kHz and 0.01Hz at open circuit potential. For the EIS data analysis ZView software was used.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis of ASSCs was performed at different scan rates in the potential range of 0–1V. Galvanostatic charge–discharge (CD) analyses were performed at different current densities (1–15Ag−1) in the same potential range of 0–1V. The cycling stability of the ASSC device was tested by the galvanostatic charge–discharge method cycled at 5Ag−1 current density for 10,000 continuous cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation and characterization of porous N-doped reduced graphene (N-pGr)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 illustrates the stepwise synthesis of porous N-doped reduced graphene (N-pGr). N-pGr was synthesized by H2O2 treatment of rGO followed by hydrothermal treatment of the obtained pGr with liquid ammonia. Initially, the formation of pGr will take place via the oxidation of pending epoxy groups on rGO by H2O2 and further hydrolysis to hydroxyl functional groups with the parallel breaking of graphene C–C bonds [44,45].

The morphology of the formed N-pGr is investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2a and 2b represent the TEM images of rGO before and after treatment with H2O2, respectively, which clearly indicates the surface change. TEM image of rGO (Fig. 2a) indicates the transparent scrambled sheet without any pores on the surface, whereas, the pGr is clearly showing the formation of uniform pores over the surface and surface change due to the oxidation process (Fig. 2b and c). The observed size of the pores is around ∼4–6nm. Furthermore, Fig. 2d and e depict the TEM images of N-pGr displaying the uniformly distributed pores over the surface with a pore size of ∼4–6nm, as observed in pGr.

In addition, the crystalline nature of N-pGr is investigated by the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) analysis (Fig. 2e, Inset). The calculated d-spacing value from the SAED is attributed to the (002) graphitic plane of the N-pGr. The formation of pores on the surface of rGO generates GQDs, as shown in Figs. 1 and 2f. The size of the etched GQDs (3–5nm) is in good accordance with the obtained ∼4–6nm pore size of the pGr and N-pGr, which clearly indicates that the derived GQDs are from the rGO surface.

Furthermore, the created pores and the pore size in the N-pGr have been confirmed by BET surface area and pore size distribution analyses (Fig. 3 ). Fig. 3a and c shows a 206.62m2 g−1 BET surface area of the synthesized reduced graphene oxide. However, Fig. 3a and d indicates decreased BET surface area of N-pGr to 179.60m2 g−1. The reduced surface area of the N-pGr might be due to the restacking of few of the graphene layers during the synthesis of the N-pGr during the oxidative (H2O2 treatment) and subsequent nitrogen doping processes. The restacking of the graphene layers might also be due to the interactions between the inherent functional groups and the doped nitrogen. The detailed explanation is provided in subsequent section of Raman analysis. Moreover, the observed trend of the BET surface area has already been reported in the literature [43,44]. In addition, Fig. 3b displays the comparative pore size distribution for the rGO and N-pGr in which the rGO sample is showing very low porosity in comparison to N-pGr. The N-pGr sample exhibits an average pore size of 3–5nm which is satisfying well with the TEM image observation. Similarly, in the above-mentioned reports, the generated pore size was observed in the range of 4–6nm. In our study, we also followed the similar oxidative process for the generation of pores which resulted into similar pore size features. Moreover, to avoid the blockage of pores in N-pGr which can arise from using carbon containing nitrogen sources such as urea (NH2CONH2), cyanuric acid (C3H3N3O3), or melamine (C3H6N6), we opted for the use of liquid ammonia as a nitrogen precursor.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses furthermore underlined the crystalline nature of N-pGr and GQDs materials (Fig. 4a). The intense peak at ∼26° belongs to the (002) graphitic plane, which matches well with the plane observed in the SAED pattern (Fig. 2e, inset). The similar graphitic plane position for rGO, pGr, and N-pGr indicates that there are no changes in the size of the graphene sheets. However, the XRD pattern of GQDs exhibits a broad peak at 24.08° (Shown in Fig. S1a, Supporting Information), indicating the formation of small size GQDs. Moreover, XRD peak observed near ∼44° corresponds to the (100) plane, originating from the restacking of graphene sheets which is higher in case of N-pGr. The observed restacking of graphene sheets during H2O2 treatment and N2-doping is already observed in the BET analysis because the specific surface area of N-pGr decreased in comparison to that of rGO.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses furthermore underlined the crystalline nature of N-pGr and GQDs materials (Fig. 4a). The intense peak at ∼26° belongs to the (002) graphitic plane, which matches well with the plane observed in the SAED pattern (Fig. 2e, inset). The similar graphitic plane position for rGO, pGr, and N-pGr indicates that there are no changes in the size of the graphene sheets. However, the XRD pattern of GQDs exhibits a broad peak at 24.08° (Shown in Fig. S1a, Supporting Information), indicating the formation of small size GQDs. Moreover, XRD peak observed near ∼44° corresponds to the (100) plane, originating from the restacking of graphene sheets which is higher in case of N-pGr. The observed restacking of graphene sheets during H2O2 treatment and N2-doping is already observed in the BET analysis because the specific surface area of N-pGr decreased in comparison to that of rGO.

In order to check the graphitic nature of the prepared materials and introduced defects in the graphene framework arising from H2O2 treatment and N-doping, Raman analyses were performed (Fig. 4b). The observed peak at 1585–1590cm−1 corresponds to the G band, which tells about the graphitic nature of the carbon materials. The corresponding G peak is mainly derived from the C–C bond stretching of E2g vibrational mode. Moreover, the peak in the range 1338–1347cm−1 corresponds to the D band, suggesting the presence of defects in the carbon material. Mainly, the defective peak is due to the A1g vibrational mode of C. The introduced defects in pGr and N-pGr samples have been predicted by checking the respective I D/I G ratio. The I D/I G ratio for rGO was found to be 0.90, which is increased in the case of pGr to 0.93. The observed increment in defects of pGr in comparison to rGO could be due to the generated pores and the disturbance of unsaturation at their edge. In the case of N-pGr, the I D/I G ratio further increased to 1.05, which is clearly revealing the hetero atom doping, which involves the increase of defects by deformation or changing the bond characteristics and bond strength with neighbouring carbon atoms. The I D/I G ratio for GQDs was also determined and found to be 0.95 (Shown in Fig. S2b, Supporting Information).

In order to check the graphitic nature of the prepared materials and introduced defects in the graphene framework arising from H2O2 treatment and N-doping, Raman analyses was performed (Fig. 4b). The observed peak at 1585–1590cm−1 corresponds to the G band, which tells about the graphitic nature of the carbon materials. The corresponding G peak is mainly derived from the C–C bond stretching of E2g vibrational mode. Moreover, the peak in the range 1338–1347cm−1 corresponds to the D band, suggesting the presence of defects in the carbon material. Mainly, the defective peak is due to the A1g vibrational mode of C. The introduced defects in pGr and N-pGr samples have been predicted by checking the respective I D/I G ratio. The I D/I G ratio for rGO was found to be 0.90, which is increased in the case of pGr to 0.93. The observed increment in defects of pGr in comparison to rGO could be due to the generated pores and the disturbance of unsaturation at their edge. In the case of N-pGr, the I D/I G ratio further increased to 1.05, which is clearly revealing the hetero atom doping, which involves the increase of defects by deformation or changing the bond characteristics and bond strength with neighbouring carbon atoms. The I D/I G ratio for GQDs was also determined and found to be 0.95 (Shown in Fig. S2b, Supporting Information).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was further employed to get information about the chemical composition of N-pGr as well as to estimate the amount of nitrogen dopant (Fig. 4c). The XPS survey scan spectrum of N-pGr shows contributions due to C1s (∼285eV), O1s (∼532eV) and N1s (∼400eV) with 5.92at.% nitrogen. Fig. 4d depicts the comparative deconvoluted C1s spectra of the pGr and N-pGr in which the sample N-pGr is showing extra deconvoluted peaks for the N-C bonds coming from the doped-N. After the deconvolution of C1s spectra of N-pGr, it is clearly showing two separated intense peaks at 285.87 (–CN) and 287.31eV (–C–N) which evidenced the doping of N in the graphene framework. Moreover, the deconvolution of the N1s spectrum of N-pGr (Fig. 4e) exhibits contributions at 398.90, 399.71 and 400.51eV due to the pyridinic-N (45.30%), pyrrolic-N (36.26%) and quaternary-N (18.44%) nitrogens, respectively [46]. In the deconvoluted N1s spectrum the amount of pyridinic-N is found to be higher than the other two components. The higher atomic percentage of pyridinic-N in the N-pGr might contribute towards the better performance of the ASSC device as suggested in the literature [34,47,48]. It is believed that, N dopants in graphene act as electron donors resulting into a band shift to accommodate extra electrons. Comparison of the electronic structure of N-doped graphene, including pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogens, showed that the pyridinic groups have the strongest electron deficiency for accepting electrons. In addition, N-doped graphene sheets with pyridinic-like hexagonal holes further allow for fast diffusion rate for electrolyte ions [34,47,48].

The capacitance of the fabricated all solid state supercapacitor (ASSC) devices with either rGO, pGr or N-pGr as active electrode material was studied by using cyclic voltammetry (CV), EIS measurement and galvanostatic charge–discharge (CD). Fig. 5a compares the CVs of the ASSC devices fabricated with rGO, pGr and N-pGr active materials at 10mVs−1. All the 3-devices display square-type CV profiles, which indicates the pure contribution of electrical double layer capacitance (Fig. 5a). However, the N-pGr based device shows an additional small redox behaviour at 0.3–0.4V, resulting most likely from N-doping [34,35,49]. The specific capacitance (C s) of the ASSC devices from CV profile was calculated by using Eq. (1): [50]
(1)
C s
= 2 ×
A r e a
o f
t h e
c a t h o d i c
C V
c u r v e
(
E 1
−
E 2
∗ v ∗ m )
where “E 1–E 2” is the potential window in volts, “m” is the corresponding mass of the electrode active material, and “v” is the scan rate.

The rGO in the fabricated ASSC device exhibited a specific capacitance of 78Fg−1 at 10mVs−1 which increased to 114Fg−1 for devices fabricated with pGr. However, the obtained specific surface area for the rGO is higher in comparison to that of N-pGr but there an increase in capacitance has been observed in case of N-pGr. The observed higher surface area of the rGO might be due to the interlayer spaces between the graphene layers. Moreover, the observed increase in the capacitance from rGO to N-pGr might be due to the better accessibility of electrolyte ions to the electrode surface, due to the presence of pores in the graphene structure. The capacitance significantly increased to 218Fg−1 after nitrogen doping, which is in good agreement with the Zhao et al. [34] report. In the above mentioned report it was observed theoretically that the porous graphene electrode with moderate pore diameter of 4.2–10Å facilitates efficient access of electrolyte and exhibits excellent rate capability. In addition, the N dopants act as electron donors or proton attractors which leads to charge accumulation and involved in higher pseudo capacitance.

To validate the performance of the N-pGr based ASSC devices, CVs at various scan rates were performed (Fig. 5b). The rectangular CV curve was retained even at higher scan rates. The observed CV behaviour indicates lower resistance of the device along with good accessibility of electrolyte ions to the electrode. This is in contrast to ASSC devices, fabricated by using rGO or pGr (see Fig. S2, Supporting Information) where the retention of capacitance is observed to be poor at higher charge–discharge current density along with an Ohmic behaviour at higher scan rates.

To validate the performance of the N-pGr based ASSC devices, CVs at various scan rates were performed (Fig. 5b). The rectangular CV curve was retained even at higher scan rates. The observed CV behaviour indicates lower resistance of the device along with good accessibility of electrolyte ions to the electrode. This is in contrast to ASSC devices, fabricated by using rGO or pGr (see Fig. S2, Supporting Information) where the retention of capacitance is observed to be poor at higher charge–discharge current density along with an Ohmic behaviour at higher scan rates.

In order to support the explanations given for the improved capacitance of ASSC device fabricated with N-pGr electrode active material due to fast ion transport, EIS analysis was carried out. Fig. 5c shows the respective Nyquist plots. The rGO shows a poor capacitive behaviour in comparison to pGr and N-pGr. Among the 3-devices, the ASSC fabricated with N-pGr has the smallest serial resistance (R s ∼1.2Ω). The high frequency region shows a small semicircle due to charge transfer resistance (R CT) from the electrode-electrolyte interface. The observed charge transfer resistance originates from the electrical contact of electrolyte to the electrode material and ionic movement. The observed lower R CT of the N-pGr is clarifying the easy access of the electrolyte to the porous N-doped graphene and the outperformed behaviour of the N-pGr device.

Moreover, this also indicates that the observed capacitance is a combination of double layer and oxidation of carbon during the potential sweep. The capacitance obtained from the CV measurement for N-pGr device was further evidenced by galvanostatic charge–discharge (CD) measurements. Fig. 5d shows the charge–discharge measurements of the N-pGr device in the potential range of 0–1V at different current densities (1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 10 and 15Ag−1). The capacitance of the N-pGr electrode in two electrode ASSC device was determined from the charge–discharge profile by using the following equation 2: [25,50]
(2)
C s
= 2 ×
j t
Δ
V
where “j” corresponds to the discharge current density, “t” is the discharge time and “ΔV” is the potential range of charge–discharge. The N-pGr electrode displayed a capacitance of 230Fg−1 at 1Ag−1, validating the obtained capacitance value from the CV measurements. Fig. 5e depicts the change in capacitance upon the application of different current densities. The initial capacitance of 230Fg−1 at 1Ag−1 decreased to 146Fg−1 at higher current density of 15Ag−1. However, the rGO based ASSC device has shown very low retention of capacitance with current density. Whereas, the pGr based device exhibited comparable retention of capacitance to the N-pGr (Shown in Fig. S2, Supporting Information). The observed decrease in capacitance with increasing current density is due to the faster polarization–depolarization of electrodes which reduces the complete utilization of electrode surface for the electrical double layer formation at higher current density. In addition, due to the higher applied charge–discharge current density when the movement of ions will be faster the electrolyte ions will not access all the surface area of the electrode. The capacitance retention for the N-pGr device is still found to be comparable to the literature values and the obtained capacitance at higher current density is credited to the porous nature of N-pGr, which is providing an easy way to the electrolyte ions for accessing the electrode surfaces.

Moreover, this also indicates that the observed capacitance is a combination of double layer and oxidation of carbon during the potential sweep. The capacitance obtained from the CV measurement for N-pGr device was further evidenced by galvanostatic charge–discharge (CD) measurements. Fig. 5d shows the charge–discharge measurements of the N-pGr device in the potential range of 0–1V at different current densities (1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 10 and 15Ag−1). The capacitance of the N-pGr electrode in two electrode ASSC device was determined from the charge–discharge profile by using the following equation 2: [25,50]
(2)
C s
= 2 ×
j t
Δ
V
where “j” corresponds to the discharge current density, “t” is the discharge time and “ΔV” is the potential range of charge–discharge. The N-pGr electrode displayed a capacitance of 230Fg−1 at 1Ag−1, validating the obtained capacitance value from the CV measurements. Fig. 5e depicts the change in capacitance upon the application of different current densities. The initial capacitance of 230Fg−1 at 1Ag−1 decreased to 146Fg−1 at higher current density of 15Ag−1. However, the rGO based ASSC device has shown very low retention of capacitance with current density. Whereas, the pGr based device exhibited comparable retention of capacitance to the N-pGr (Shown in Fig. S2, Supporting Information). The observed decrease in capacitance with increasing current density is due to the faster polarization–depolarization of electrodes which reduces the complete utilization of electrode surface for the electrical double layer formation at higher current density. In addition, due to the higher applied charge–discharge current density when the movement of ions will be faster the electrolyte ions will not access all the surface area of the electrode. The capacitance retention for the N-pGr device is still found to be comparable to the literature values and the obtained capacitance at higher current density is credited to the porous nature of N-pGr, which is providing an easy way to the electrolyte ions for accessing the electrode surfaces.

The longer cycle life at higher charge–discharge current density is an important criterion to judge the electrochemical performance of SCs. The electrochemical durability of the fabricated ASSC device was checked by galvanostatic charge–discharge cycling for 10,000 cycles at a current density of 5Ag−1 (Fig. 5f). A 12% decrease in capacitance is observed after 10,000 continuous cycling, revealing the higher stability of the electrode material. The observed decrease in capacitance of the device may originate from the electrolysis of water molecules used in gel electrolyte. Furthermore, to see if the fabricated supercapacitor device can be used for real applications, the lightening of a LED which operates at 2.5V is demonstrated (Fig. 5f, Inset). For lighting the LED, 3 supercapacitor devices were connected in series, to be able to operate 3V. After the connection of LED to fully charged supercapacitor devices, LED starts illuminating (Fig. 5f, Inset and Movie S1, Supporting Information).

The longer cycle life at higher charge–discharge current density is an important criteria to judge the electrochemical performance of SCs. The electrochemical durability of the fabricated ASSC device was checked by galvanostatic charge–discharge cycling for 10,000 cycles at a current density of 5Ag−1 (Fig. 5f). A 12% decrease in capacitance is observed after 10,000 continuous cycling, revealing the higher stability of the electrode material. The observed decrease in capacitance of the device may originate from the electrolysis of water molecules used in gel electrolyte. Furthermore, to see if the fabricated supercapacitor device can be used for real applications, the lightening of a LED which operates at 2.5V is demonstrated (Fig. 5f, Inset). For lighting the LED, 3 supercapacitor devices were connected in series, to be able to operate 3V. After the connection of LED to fully charged supercapacitor devices, LED starts illuminating (Fig. 5f, Inset and Movie S1, Supporting Information).

The energy density (E d) and power density (P d) of the ASSC device fabricated with N-pGr were further calculated by using the capacitance obtained at different current densities from the charge–discharge curves (for calculation see Supporting Information), shown in Fig. 6a. The N-pGr material exhibits an energy density and power density of 7.98Whkg−1 and 0.27kWkg−1, respectively at a charge–discharge current density of 1Ag−1. Furthermore, the ASSC device displays an energy density of 4.93Whkg−1 even at high power density of 4.07Wkg−1. These results support well the importance of pores and nitrogen doping in N-pGr electrode active material for the fabrication of a high performance supercapacitor device. Furthermore, the performance of the ASSC device fabricated by using N-pGr as an electrode active material has been compared with the reported N-doped carbon based materials for the supercapacitor application (Table 1). The reported capacitance 230Fg−1 for the ASSC device fabricated with N-pGr device is having a comparable value as reported for the hetero-atom doped systems. However, most of the reports tabulated in Table 1 are tested in the liquid state and charge storage ability and stability were recorded at lower current density. Our fabricated device with N-pGr has shown superior performance in comparison to most of the carbon material based devices reported in the literature in terms of capacitance as well as durability.

The energy density (E d) and power density (P d) of the ASSC device fabricated with N-pGr was further calculated by using the capacitance obtained at different current densities from the charge–discharge curves (for calculation see Supporting Information), shown in Fig. 6a. The N-pGr material exhibits an energy density and power density of 7.98Whkg−1 and 0.27kWkg−1, respectively at a charge–discharge current density of 1Ag−1. Furthermore, the ASSC device displays an energy density of 4.93Whkg−1 even at high power density of 4.07Wkg−1. These results support well the importance of pores and nitrogen doping in N-pGr electrode active material for the fabrication of a high performance supercapacitor device. Furthermore, the performance of the ASSC device fabricated by using N-pGr as an electrode active material has been compared with the reported N-doped carbon based materials for the supercapacitor application (Table 1 ). The reported capacitance 230Fg−1 for the ASSC device fabricated with N-pGr device is having a comparable value as reported for the hetero-atom doped systems. However, most of the reports tabulated in Table 1 are tested in the liquid state and charge storage ability and stability were recorded at lower current density. Our fabricated device with N-pGr has shown superior performance in comparison to most of the carbon material based devices reported in the literature in terms of capacitance as well as durability.

Furthermore, by looking at the importance of flexible energy devices for the modern day electronic gadgets, the same material (N-pGr) was used to fabricate a flexible solid-state supercapacitor (FSSC). For the fabrication of FSSC Grafoil® carbon paper was used as a current collector. Fig. 6b shows the capacitive CV profile of the FSSC device fabricated with N-pGr at different bending conditions as shown in the inset of Fig. 6b. The capacitive CV shows that the electrochemical performance of the FSSC device is not deteriorated even after bending at higher angles. The electrical performance of the FSSC device clearly reveals the robustness of the supercapacitor device. Since the method adopted for the fabrication of high performance ASSC and FSSC is simple and scalable, it represents a more practical way to make SC devices.

In summary, we have synthesized N-pGr by using a simple and scalable method. The prepared N-pGr was utilized for the fabrication of ASSC and FSSC devices which performed better than its counterpart synthesized materials (rGO and pGr). The synergistic effect of the porosity and N-doping, results in the better charge storage ability. N-pGr electrode exhibited a capacitance of 230Fg−1 at 1Ag−1 current density with excellent cycling durability up to 10,000 cycles. Importantly, the porous nature of N-pGr contributes in increasing the electroactive surface area for the formation of electrical double layer and also facilitates the faster movement of electrolyte ions inside the electrode network. The N-doping in N-pGr is believed to change the electrical properties of the electrode material and also to participate in the pseudo-capacitance which helped to boost the overall capacitance of the fabricated device. The good capacitance retention in its FSSC state provides the room for making of robust FSSC with the N-pGr material.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: SKS is thankful to CEFIPRA and Campus France for providing fellowship under Raman-Charpak fellowship scheme. SS and RB acknowledge financial support from the Centre National de la RechercheScientifique (CNRS), Lille1 University, and Hauts-de-France region. SKS and SK gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), CSIR-National Chemical Laboratory (CSIR-NCL).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.apmt.2016.10.002.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:
